persons.arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: the stint belongs to a same-name relative (a son or brother, also 'J. E. Dombreux') serving as a district clerk in Mauritius 1879-1883, while the biographee held a different district post. This fails because the biography itself places the biographee in exactly this office during exactly this window: he was district clerk at Plaines Wilhelms from 18th April 1876 and 'now at Black River' in the 1888 edition, so he was a serving district clerk in the Mauritius District Magistracy continuously across 1879-1883. For the counter-hypothesis to hold there would have to be TWO simultaneous 'J. E. Dombreux' district clerks in the small Mauritius establishment, yet the staff lists show only one such entry per year and the corpus contains only this one biography for the surname. The name difference (DOMBREU vs Dombreux) is a trailing-letter OCR/typesetting variant, not evidence of a different person. The deterministic FAILs are mechanical artifacts: position_sim and tenure_overlap failed only because the compiled timeline carries no explicit year range spanning 1879-1883, but the verbatim 1888 entry text makes the continuity ('1876 ... now at Black River') obvious.

evidence_cited.fact: district clerk at Pamplemousses, 1863, at Plaines Wilhelms, 18th April, 1876, now at Black River

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous J. E. Dombreux in the Mauritius district magistracy (e.g. two Dombreux entries in the same staff-list year, or a second biography), or a staff-list district assignment for this stint contradicting Plaines Wilhelms/Black River in 1879-1883.

unassigned_stints.reason: The printed staff-list records for this stint read 'J. Dombreux' (single initial, no 'E.') and the position is '2nd Class Master' at the 'Royal College School' - a schoolteaching post with no connection to the biographee's 30-year career as procureur-general's clerk and district clerk, who per the 1888 edition was still a district clerk 'now at Black River'. An abrupt 1889 switch from senior district clerk to junior (2nd class) schoolmaster is implausible career shape; a same-surname relative (e.g. a son named J. Dombreux) is at least as likely - the corpus notes 2 officials with this surname in the staff lists. No position, department, honour, or edition dimension supports the link, so it stays unassigned.

notes: The two stints are almost certainly two different people: the District Magistracy clerk (the biographee) and a Royal College schoolmaster printed with a single initial 'J.'. The stint key 'Dombreux, J. E___Mauritius___1889' carries 'J. E' from the blocking pass, but the year-by-year records show only 'J. Dombreux', which weakens the initials check the deterministic pass marked as PASS.
